did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) model for the classification of skin cancer into multiple classes using the HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets. It also employed transfer learning models (ResNet152V2, VGG16, and EfficientNetB7) for comparative analysis and quantified racial bias in the datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the development of a proposed Parallel Residual Convolution Network model, performance evaluation on HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets (achieving 98.55% and 92.71% accuracy respectively), and a quantitative analysis of racial bias. Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is reporting new results and model validation, classifying it as a research paper. The ACM reference format indicates it was published in the '3rd International Conference on Computing Advancements (ICCA 2024)', confirming it is a conference research paper.

title: Bias in Deep Learning Skin Cancer Detection: Parallel Residual Convolution Network Classification and Racial Bias Quantification: Skin Cancer Racial Bias
brief_summary: This study addresses racial bias in deep learning-based skin cancer detection by developing a Parallel Residual Convolution Network (DCNN) for multi-class classification. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets, achieving classification accuracies of 98.55% and 92.71%, respectively. The authors also performed a quantitative analysis of racial bias by examining the brightness and color intensity distributions within these datasets. Comparative performance evaluations were conducted against pre-trained transfer learning models, including ResNet152V2, VGG16, and EfficientNetB7. The results indicated that ResNet152V2 performed best among the transfer learning models, while the proposed DCNN demonstrated high efficacy. The study highlights the presence of bias towards lighter skin tones in the datasets and emphasizes the need for more inclusive data collection to ensure fair healthcare outcomes.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model is intended for 'thorough categorization of skin cancer in several classes' and 'multiclass skin cancer recognition'. It reports classification accuracy (98.55% on HAM10000, 92.71% on ISIC-2019), precision, recall, and F1 scores. The final output layer uses a softmax activation function with seven neurons corresponding to the lesion classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing 10,050 dermoscopic images representing seven different classes for detecting pigmented skin lesions. It is freely accessible through the ISIC archive.

use.training: Used for training the proposed Parallel Residual Convolution Network (DCNN) and for evaluating pre-trained transfer learning models (ResNet152V2, VGG16, EfficientNetB7).
use.validation: Used for validation during model training and evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing the model, achieving 98.55% accuracy and 0.9855 F1 score.
use.other: Used for racial bias quantification analysis (brightness and color intensity).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A collection of 25,331 images displaying eight kinds of skin lesions. It includes image metadata such as patient location, age, and sex. The dataset includes a test dataset with 8,239 pictures and a training dataset with an extra outlier class.

use.training: Used for training the proposed Parallel Residual Convolution Network (DCNN) and for evaluating pre-trained transfer learning models.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training and evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing the model, achieving 92.71% accuracy and 0.9345 F1 score.
use.other: Used for racial bias quantification analysis (brightness and color intensity).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance by summarizing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for each class.
To quantify the classification performance of the proposed model and transfer learning models (ResNet152V2, VGG-16, EfficientNetB7) on HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets.
To quantify racial bias by measuring the overall luminance of images in the datasets, comparing distributions against reference values for white, brown, and black skin tones.
To quantify racial bias by identifying the dominant skin tone (RGB values) in each image and visualizing the distribution to detect overrepresentation of lighter skin tones.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Racial bias was quantified using descriptive statistics (average brightness and mode color intensity) and compared against reference values for skin tones, but no statistical significance testing was performed.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed model achieved the highest performance among all compared models. The confusion matrix for HAM10000 showed high true positive counts for Melanocytic nevi (1359), Melanoma (1318), Benign Keratosis (1261), Basal Cell Carcinoma (1351), Vascular Lesion (1246), Actinic Keratoses (1358), and Dermatofibroma (1358).

ISIC-2019.notes: The proposed model outperformed existing models and transfer learning baselines on this dataset. ResNet152V2 performed best among the transfer learning models.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported for the proposed model; transfer learning models (ResNet152V2, VGG-16, EfficientNetB7) were used for comparison.

backbone_components: Initial convolutional layer with 32 filters of size 3x3
Three sequential 'blockred' modules increasing filter count to 64

Hybrid block ('blockred') integrating: Inception modules (1x1 convolution, max-pooling, batch normalization), VGG-style convolutions with SqueezeNet principles (consecutive 3x3 convolutions), and Inception-ResNet techniques (3x3 and 1x1 convolutions with max-pooling and batch normalization)
regularization: Dropout layers inserted after each fully connected layer

training_details.data_split: 80:20 ratio for training and testing-validation sets
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Oversampling of minority classes using RandomOverSampler

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully Connected Layers followed by Softmax

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resizing: Images were resized to 28x28 pixels with 3 color channels (RGB) for the HAM10000 dataset.

Image Rescaling: Mean pixel value of the total dataset was subtracted from every pixel value, and resulting values were divided by the standard deviation of the dataset to align data to nearly zero and reduce pixel bias.
Oversampling: Minority classes were oversampled using RandomOverSampler to balance sample distribution across classes.
Image Augmentation: Applied rotation, shearing, zooming, horizontal flipping, ranging of height and width, and resizing on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
Data Splitting: The augmented dataset was split into training and testing-validation sets with an 80:20 ratio.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the ISIC 2019 dataset; images were resized to 28x28 pixels with 3 color channels (RGB).
Used to balance sample distribution across classes by randomly reproducing samples of minority classes until each class is sufficiently represented.

conclusions: The proposed Parallel Residual Convolution Network (DCNN) achieved high classification accuracy of 98.55% on the HAM10000 dataset and 92.71% on the ISIC-2019 dataset, outperforming existing models and transfer learning baselines.
Among the evaluated transfer learning models (ResNet152V2, VGG16, and EfficientNetB7), ResNet152V2 demonstrated the best performance.

Quantitative analysis revealed significant racial bias in both the HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets, characterized by an overrepresentation of lighter skin tones (higher average brightness and mode color intensity clustering around values typical of white skin).
The study highlights that discrepancies in skin tone representation within datasets negatively influence the development of machine-learning algorithms for skin cancer detection.
The authors conclude that future research must focus on using more diverse datasets and advanced techniques, such as attention mechanisms, to mitigate racial bias and ensure fair healthcare outcomes.
important_remarks: The study quantifies racial bias in the HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets by analyzing average brightness and mode color intensity, revealing a significant overrepresentation of lighter skin tones (clustering around brightness/color values typical of white skin).

The proposed Parallel Residual Convolution Network achieved high accuracy (98.55% on HAM10000, 92.71% on ISIC-2019), but these results are derived from datasets with known racial bias, potentially limiting generalizability to darker skin tones.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) for the racial bias quantification; bias is assessed using descriptive statistics (average brightness and mode color intensity) compared against reference values.
The model was trained on a dataset with class imbalance, which was addressed via oversampling (RandomOverSampler) of minority classes, a technique that may introduce synthetic data artifacts.
The study lacks external validation on diverse, independent datasets with balanced racial representation, relying solely on HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 which are known to have demographic skew.

The input image resolution was reduced to 28x28 pixels, which may discard fine-grained dermoscopic features critical for accurate skin cancer diagnosis, particularly for subtle lesion characteristics.

The paper acknowledges that discrepancies in skin tone representation negatively influence machine-learning algorithm development, highlighting a critical limitation in current public datasets for clinical deployment fairness.
